quotes.quote: We don't associate the word 'robber' with billionaires because the wealthy have had millennia to cement the association of wealth with class.
quotes.summary: Shaw coins the term 'Beggar Barons' to describe trillion-dollar tech companies that exploit open source developers by begging for free labor instead of paying for it. Drawing a parallel to the Robber Barons of the 1800s, he argues that companies like Apple, Microsoft, and Google built their empires on unpaid open source contributions while maintaining a facade of innovation. He illustrates this with personal anecdotes: Apple begging him to modify Mongrel for free, Microsoft expecting free documentation fixes via pull requests, and Microsoft attempting to absorb his website's traffic upon hiring him. Shaw formally defines Beggar Barons as billion-dollar companies that use open source as a facade to extract free labor they could easily afford to pay for.
quotes.key_insight: Trillion-dollar tech companies have replaced industrial-era robbery with a subtler exploitation: using social manipulation and open source ideology to beg developers into donating labor that these companies could trivially afford to pay for.

quotes.quote: These survival crafting games were a coping mechanism for a situation where I was helpless.
quotes.summary: Shaw recounts how survival crafting video games became his primary coping mechanism during the COVID-19 pandemic. Starting from a place of helplessness and despair — alone in his apartment, uncertain about the virus, and feeling powerless against forces beyond his control — he dove into a series of games (Windbound, Subnautica, The Forest, 7 Days to Die, Valheim) where he was similarly alone and vulnerable but could actually fight back. He progressed from hating death in games to embracing it, learning to mod games to reject unfair mechanics and take control. When he received his vaccine, his obsession with the games evaporated, revealing they had been a psychological survival mechanism mirroring his real-world situation.
quotes.key_insight: Survival crafting games served as a psychological pressure valve during the pandemic by giving Shaw a fantasy version of his real helplessness — one where he could actually fight back, take control, and make dying fun.

quotes.quote: In the games I could change the situation, adapt my environment, get stronger, and beat the invisible hand that was trying to destroy me.

quotes.quote: The knife is justice. Retribution. Correction.
quotes.summary: Shaw tells the story of receiving a custom titanium palette knife he ordered four years ago and forgot about, then immediately damaging it through an unnecessary repair attempt. He applied permanent Locktite to a perfectly fine screw, couldn't remove it, and scored the polished titanium pommel with pliers. He then redeemed himself by progressively sanding and polishing the damage away, making a creative connection between titanium polishing and a guitar fret polishing cloth he had lying around. The story serves as a self-portrait of his personality: impulsive destruction followed by resourceful repair.
quotes.key_insight: Shaw's identity is the cycle of impulsive destruction and resourceful repair — the same recklessness that creates problems also enables the cross-domain thinking that solves them.

quotes.quote: It's my belief that authoritarianism is so ingrained in the fabric of software development that it has been internalized to such a degree that even the most anti-authoritarian people cannot avoid being authoritarian.
quotes.summary: Shaw argues that authoritarianism is deeply embedded in software development communities, particularly open source projects. He defines authoritarianism as deference to authority given or taken without enthusiastic informed consent, and separates analysis of authoritarian leaders from authoritarian communities. Using the Python community as his primary case study, he contrasts the treatment of PSF leader Steve Holden—who allegedly asked women to touch a sexually suggestive prop at PyCon 2012 and faced no consequences—with the immediate ejection and job losses of two attendees who made a private dongle joke at PyCon 2013. Shaw contends this disparity in punishment demonstrates clear deference to authority, proving the Python community and its leadership operate as an authoritarian system.
quotes.key_insight: Open source communities that claim to be meritocratic and egalitarian often function as authoritarian systems where rules are enforced harshly on ordinary members but waived for those in positions of power, and the community's willingness to accept this double standard is itself the core mechanism of authoritarianism.

quotes.quote: Google doesn't actually want to help the poor. Google wants to just look like they're helping the poor with the least probability that anyone who's actually poor would gain any amount of power.
quotes.summary: Shaw argues that Google's free programming courses marketed as bachelor's degree equivalents are performative rather than genuinely helpful to the poor, noting that Google ignored community colleges and didn't offer free MBA programs that would give actual power to disadvantaged people. He then turns to what he calls 'Empathy Gatekeepers'—people who demand programmers take extensive ethics and soft skills courses while ignoring the complete absence of such requirements in Google's program. Shaw contends that demands for programmer ethics training are really about control and political signaling, not about producing better people, pointing out that history's worst actors were trained in soft skills, not programming. He concludes that if ethics advocates were serious, they'd hold corporations and managers accountable first rather than targeting powerless working programmers.
quotes.key_insight: Demands for programmer ethics education are a form of power politics that targets workers with no institutional power while giving a pass to the corporations and managers who actually make unethical decisions.

quotes.summary: Shaw argues that Google's free programming courses marketed as bachelor's degree equivalents are performative rather than genuinely helpful to the poor, noting that Google ignored community colleges and didn't offer free MBA programs that would give actual power to disadvantaged people. He then turns to what he calls 'Empathy Gatekeepers'—people who demand programmers take extensive ethics and soft skills courses while ignoring the complete absence of such requirements in Google's program. Shaw contends that demands for programmer ethics training are really about control and political signaling, not about producing better people, pointing out that history's worst actors were trained in soft skills, not programming. He concludes that if ethics advocates were serious, they'd hold corporations and managers accountable first rather than targeting powerless working programmers.
quotes.key_insight: Demands for programmer ethics education are a form of power politics that targets workers with no institutional power while giving a pass to the corporations and managers who actually make unethical decisions.

quotes.quote: For the last 30 years nearly every major movement in software has been an attempt to shame programmers into feeling inadequate for a situation that is entire out of their control.
quotes.summary: Shaw draws a parallel between the fear-driven perfectionism he observed in classical realist painters and the shame-based culture of the programming industry. He argues that for 30 years, corporations and open source leaders have weaponized shame to control programmers, creating an internalized authoritarianism that squeezes creative space out of code. This realization crystallizes when he encounters Nim's style insensitivity feature, which allows flexible naming conventions, and has a visceral negative reaction he can't rationally justify. He recognizes his reaction as the same authoritarian impulse he criticizes, and resolves to try Nim as an act of defiance against rigid programming culture. The post connects his artistic journey toward leaving space in paintings with a desire for the same creative freedom in programming.
quotes.key_insight: The programming industry's culture of shame and rigid correctness is an internalized authoritarianism that kills creative expression, and recognizing it in yourself is the first step toward writing code with the same freedom you'd bring to art.

quotes.quote: I desperately needed to keep going outside and painting. It was my white whale. My dragon. I had to chase the elusive experience of painting in the perfect spot at the perfect time.
quotes.summary: Shaw recounts a plein air painting expedition through Tbilisi, Georgia during a round-the-world trip, walking miles on a repeatedly injured left ankle while carrying 25 pounds of painting and camera gear. He describes his obsessive dedication to outdoor painting as a personal compulsion, detailing his equipment setup, the Georgian food and culture he fell in love with, and his discovery of the perfect painting spot on a pedestrian walkway near the Bridge of Peace. The piece builds from travelogue into a meditation on the emotional process of translating a scene into paint, using palette knives to capture the swirling contrast of sadness at leaving Tbilisi mixed with excitement about returning home. Written entirely from memory, it captures the intersection of physical suffering, artistic obsession, and the bittersweet end of a journey before 2020 changed everything.
quotes.key_insight: Artistic obsession overrides physical pain and practical sense, because the compulsion to capture a perfect moment in paint is its own reward that no injury can diminish.

quotes.quote: I have to focus to disable the part of my brain that is trying to nonchalantly glance at the photons entering my optic nerve and slap the word 'flower' on it then move on.
quotes.summary: Shaw explores painting as a practice of disabling the brain's automatic pattern recognition to see raw visual reality. He examines three properties of paintings—energy, space, and magic—arguing that energy comes from loose, intuitive brushwork, space determines how much room a painting leaves for viewer interpretation, and magic is a false mysticism people project onto a learnable skill. He draws an analogy between tightly defined research papers and open-ended poetry to explain how paintings can either dictate meaning or invite the viewer in. He concludes that the real 'magic' of painting is the deliberate exploitation of visual perception to create experiences, not some spiritual gift.
quotes.key_insight: Painting is not mystical talent but a learnable skill of temporarily overriding the brain's automatic perception, and its real power lies in deliberately exploiting visual cognition to create controlled experiences for viewers.

quotes.quote: Thinking back this was a terrible idea because my programming skills didn't have anywhere to exercise, and a blog is an excellent way to stay sharp.
quotes.summary: Zed Shaw documents his migration away from WordPress to a custom blog built with Svelte and Sapper. After using WordPress since 2014 while he studied painting, he grew frustrated with its poor media handling and confusing UI. He credits Svelte's reactive model and the Zettlr writing tool as the catalysts for rebuilding his web presence. The post reflects on how maintaining custom blog software keeps programming skills sharp, something he regrets neglecting during his painting years.
quotes.key_insight: Outsourcing your tools to platforms like WordPress trades short-term convenience for long-term skill atrophy and creative limitation.

quotes.quote: A man who just became a billionaire took time out of his day to hunt down a comment by a regular nobody 11 years later and throw his success back in that guy's face.
quotes.summary: Shaw defends 'BrandonM,' a Hacker News commenter whose 2007 skeptical comment about Dropbox was publicly mocked by Drew Houston and Sam Altman over a decade later. Shaw argues this represents a power imbalance where billionaires punch down at ordinary people who can't even delete their old comments. He critiques Y Combinator's refusal to allow comment deletion as abusive and contradictory to their professed progressive values. Shaw then methodically analyzes BrandonM's original comment, arguing it was actually reasonable given the 2007 technology landscape. The core argument is that Silicon Valley elites demand openness to criticism but weaponize their power against anyone who once doubted them.
quotes.key_insight: Silicon Valley billionaires weaponize old criticisms against powerless individuals while hiding behind progressive values they don't actually practice.

He critiques Y Combinator's refusal to allow comment deletion as abusive and contradictory to their professed progressive values. Shaw then methodically analyzes BrandonM's original comment, arguing it was actually reasonable given the 2007 technology landscape. The core argument is that Silicon Valley elites demand openness to criticism but weaponize their power against anyone who once doubted them.
quotes.key_insight: Silicon Valley billionaires weaponize old criticisms against powerless individuals while hiding behind progressive values they don't actually practice.

quotes.quote: When photographing artwork (or any forensic photography), your ideas of artistic integrity do not belong. You're trying to record as exactly as possible what is photographed.
quotes.summary: Zed Shaw explores the surprisingly difficult challenge of photographing paintings accurately. He explains that cameras' automatic corrections, light color temperature, specular highlights, and exposure issues all conspire to produce inaccurate reproductions. After extensive experimentation with various techniques, he settles on a process using a tripod, zoom lens, Expodisc for white balance calibration, and multiple exposures—though he admits he usually skips most of it out of laziness.
quotes.key_insight: Photographing paintings is a forensic documentation problem, not an artistic one, and most photography tools and platforms are designed to make images look pleasing rather than accurate.

quotes.quote: Rather than try to take a photo correctly the first time, a photographer will simply take a whole bunch of photos and then fix them all up in Photoshop from memory.

quotes.quote: People are so attached to their programming languages that they even hate newbies who just don't ask the right questions in IRC.
quotes.summary: Shaw addresses the death threats and suicide encouragement he receives from programming communities angered by his opinions on languages like Python 3. He traces the psychology of online abuse from IRC hostility toward beginners to escalating bullying that can drive people to suicide, drawing parallels between tech community harassment and broader patterns of sociopathic abuse. He argues that online communities collectively designate acceptable targets, giving sociopaths permission to escalate harassment. Shaw refuses to be intimidated, asserting his right to self-defense and declaring that his life is thriving despite the threats.
quotes.key_insight: Programming communities that collectively designate acceptable targets for hatred—whether beginners or dissenters—are enabling the same escalation patterns that drive real-world abuse and tragedy.

quotes.quote: Imagine thousands of Python programmers deciding that you don't deserve to work and make a living because you don't like Python 3.
quotes.summary: Zed Shaw details his conflict with the Python Software Foundation after publicly criticizing Python 3, claiming PSF members actively tried to censor his book and damage his livelihood. He outlines anticipated PSF defenses and systematically rebuts each one, arguing that the PSF's Code of Conduct is meaningless if never enforced. Shaw contends that his treatment demonstrates a broader pattern where dissenting opinions in the Python community are met with abuse rather than constructive engagement. He warns other Python developers that if the PSF is willing to target someone with his stature, smaller contributors are even more vulnerable.
quotes.key_insight: Organizations that adopt codes of conduct but never enforce them against members who harass dissenters create environments where honest criticism becomes professionally dangerous.

quotes.quote: I wouldn't say I have any particular talent at painting. I'm definitely not a genius and I put in a lot of work to be even moderately alright at it.
quotes.summary: Shaw reflects on a failed flower painting to argue that talent is irrelevant to enjoying creative pursuits. He describes his lack of natural ability at painting, explaining that his skills come entirely from reading books and deliberate practice rather than innate gift. He credits painting, particularly self-portraits and landscapes, with saving his life during a dark period. The core message is that having zero talent is actually liberating because it frees you to focus on the process rather than the result.
quotes.key_insight: Talent is neither required nor desirable for enjoying a creative pursuit — liberation comes from accepting you have none and focusing entirely on the process and what you learn.

quotes.quote: It seems impossible, but you can actually stare at a scene or a photo, and after about 2-3 minutes of staring at it, do a fairly close approximation of it.
quotes.summary: Shaw documents his experiment combining memory drawing techniques with iterative refinement processes from traditional art training. He traces the history of memory drawing back to Père Lecoq's 1848 French Academy experiments, then describes his own methodology: staring at a scene, covering it, drawing from memory, then checking accuracy and repeating with finer detail. He tests this approach across multiple media (charcoal, pastel, oil paint) and drawing styles (relative measurement, block-in, painterly), finding that pastels and the block-in/painterly combination work best. The results surprised him with their accuracy even on early rounds, and the process felt meditative compared to conventional constant-reference drawing.
quotes.key_insight: Drawing accuracy comes not from magical precision but from iterative refinement, and memory drawing combined with chunked memorization and gradual correction can match conventional methods while feeling more meditative and direct.

quotes.quote: Art is always a piece. Artists are never "popular", they're always "important".

In the first vignette, he's attacked for honestly critiquing a student's video installation piece. In the second, a teacher scolds him for painting noses as practice rather than fabricating a deep personal connection to his subject. In the third, a teacher uses distracting colored lights and music while simultaneously demanding accuracy, then admits she never uses these techniques herself. Together, these stories illustrate Shaw's frustration with art education that values pretentious narrative over craft and honest engagement.
quotes.key_insight: Art education often rewards performative emotional narratives and mystical discovery rituals while punishing students who approach their craft with honesty and practical intent.

quotes.quote: I have literally met the man for an hour and he's already insulting my gear.
quotes.summary: Shaw recounts his experience at an art school where a teacher systematically undermined him from the moment he arrived. The teacher mocked his brushes, failed to warn him about technique requirements until it was too late, and ultimately spent an entire lunch trying to convince Shaw to quit studying art—all triggered by a student's outrage over his joke Bob Ross paintings. Shaw identifies the school's culture as cult-like, where deviation from orthodoxy provokes hostile gatekeeping. He credits his own stubbornness for surviving the encounter but notes most students would have been permanently derailed.
quotes.key_insight: Art education institutions can become gatekeeping cults where teachers crush students who deviate from orthodoxy, and surviving such environments shouldn't require extraordinary personal resilience.

quotes.quote: The reality is conceptual models of education simply find students lucky enough to naturally know the topic, and then leave the rest to fail and flounder.
quotes.summary: Shaw argues that copying and repetition (rote learning) are the foundation of all education, yet computer science uniquely dismisses these methods in favor of conceptual learning. He draws parallels to music, painting, writing, and martial arts, where copying masters and repetitive practice are universally accepted as essential. He contends that conceptual-first CS education acts as a weed-out system rather than genuinely teaching beginners. Shaw proposes adopting music-style pedagogy for beginners (scales, copying, modification) and painting-style pedagogy for intermediates (master copies and studies), arguing this would make programming accessible to more people.
quotes.key_insight: Computer science education's rejection of copying and repetition in favor of conceptual learning is an anomaly among creative disciplines, and adopting the proven rote-then-create pedagogy of music and painting would make programming far more accessible to beginners.

quotes.quote: Magic just hides something's true beauty. It's a con. A trick that makes you love the magic rather than the real thing.
quotes.summary: Shaw argues that 'magic' — the mystical aura surrounding any skill or technology — is fundamentally a con that obscures reality and enslaves people. He recounts telling a musician friend that learning theory wouldn't kill her artistry but reveal something more beautiful. He extends this to technology, arguing that much of the magic around programming languages and companies hides ugly designs and poor implementations. Shaw frames his life's mission as stripping away magic through education, citing his 'Learn Code the Hard Way' books as proof that programming isn't reserved for special geniuses. He concludes that most practitioners don't intentionally gatekeep — they simply cargo-cult rituals they mistake for essential practice.
quotes.key_insight: The mystique surrounding any skill or technology serves to enslave people to gatekeepers, and education's true purpose is to strip away that mystique so anyone can access the real, simpler thing underneath.

quotes.quote: As the money in tech prolapsed into the local economy the brogrammers in the industry confused their acquisition of a loan with being real super dudes.
quotes.summary: Zed Shaw announces his move from San Francisco to Miami, reflecting on his seven years in the Bay Area with mixed feelings. He credits San Francisco for launching his book career, learning to paint, and memorable moments at Union Square and Fort Mason, but criticizes the city's toxic culture war between tech bros and anti-tech libertines. He chose Miami for its lack of state income tax (important for his plan to study art overseas), its art scene, and its generally happier vibe. The post is structured as a farewell love letter to San Francisco that doubles as an indictment of the city's personality.
quotes.key_insight: Cities shape the people who live in them, and sometimes the healthiest thing is to leave before you fully absorb a city's dysfunction.

The post is structured as a farewell love letter to San Francisco that doubles as an indictment of the city's personality.
quotes.key_insight: Cities shape the people who live in them, and sometimes the healthiest thing is to leave before you fully absorb a city's dysfunction.

quotes.quote: The reddit community responsible for teaching beginners to code censored my book as a power play to get me to force Python 3 on unsuspecting beginners.
quotes.summary: Shaw argues that the programming community's ability to influence careers and products through platforms like Reddit and Hacker News has effectively ended. He uses his own experience as evidence: after Reddit removed his Python book from recommended reading lists and online mobs attacked his criticism of Python 3, his traffic and sales remained unchanged. He contends that the previous generation of programmers consistently backed terrible ideas based on tribal loyalty rather than merit, and that a newer generation of programmers is rejecting this tribal, hive-mind culture. He concludes by encouraging creators to ignore the online mob and keep making things.
quotes.key_insight: The tech community's online outrage machine has far less real-world power than people believe, and creators should stop fearing it and build what they want.

quotes.quote: People standing around mixed with art sculptures and piles of garbage have great shapes.
quotes.summary: Shaw documents a visit to the SF MOMA where he practiced photography composition using his Sony camera's High Contrast Black & White filter. He describes his workflow of using camera effects to find interesting shapes and compositions, then capturing RAW images for painting reference. The post is primarily a photo essay with brief captions describing his approach to each shot. He focuses on using the B&W filter to abstract subjects into pure shapes, whether people, sculptures, buildings, or neon signs.
quotes.key_insight: Black and white high-contrast photography can serve as a practical composition tool for painters, stripping scenes down to their essential shapes before capturing detailed color reference.

quotes.quote: Watercolor is the inverse of other paints because it's transparent so you build paintings from light to dark, using the paper as your white "pigment".
quotes.summary: Shaw shares his practice session with watercolor and ink, explaining the fundamental challenge of watercolor: it's transparent, so you paint light to dark using the paper as white. He walks through a simple three-step process for building watercolor paintings using value layers, starting with the lightest wash and progressively adding darker tones. He experiments with adding pen and ink to color watercolors but remains unconvinced about combining the two media.
quotes.key_insight: Watercolor painting becomes approachable when you practice with monochrome ink first and think systematically about building value layers from light to dark.

quotes.quote: This project took me 6 months to complete, working on it about 8 hours a week.
quotes.summary: Shaw shares the completion of a six-month cast drawing of Saint Anger done on claybord using controlled pencil rendering. He discusses the properties of claybord as a drawing surface, noting its erasability and durability but warning it goes dark too easily. He outlines his next steps: moving to cast painting and Bargue plate copies rendered in paint rather than pencil, which he finds tedious. The post reflects a working artist methodically progressing through classical art training techniques.
quotes.key_insight: Classical art training is a methodical, long-term process where each completed project clarifies what medium and technique to pursue next.

quotes.quote: The purpose of a mastercopy is to learn how another artist would approach the subject.

He notes an imperfection in the mouth of his copy and plans to attempt another Sargent piece, possibly in pastel. He explains the pedagogical purpose of mastercopying: learning how another artist approached their subject by studying brush strokes and color mixing embedded in the work.
quotes.key_insight: Mastercopying teaches an artist to reverse-engineer another painter's technique by physically recreating their brush strokes and color decisions.

quotes.quote: People were being identified based on either a flawed sentiment analysis of how snarky they were, or a manager's assessment of whether they were being snarky.
quotes.summary: Shaw argues that Riot Games violated California employment law by mining employees' in-game chat histories without their knowledge or consent, then using that data to identify employees for termination or reprimand. He details how the company analyzed past gameplay behavior, classified employees as toxic based on dubious sentiment analysis, and planned to extend this surveillance into their hiring process. Shaw enumerates eight specific legal and ethical violations, ranging from California Labor Code 980 to human research ethics requirements to discriminatory testing laws. He concludes that the practice deserves a class action lawsuit and criticizes the tech industry for celebrating corporate surveillance.
quotes.key_insight: When a company surveils employees' personal behavior without consent and uses it for employment decisions, it's not innovative HR — it's a legal and ethical violation that the tech industry shouldn't celebrate.

quotes.quote: It's really nailing the whole Male Nerd Beta dynamic and I feel that this is an area I can contribute my ample writing chops.
quotes.summary: Shaw writes a satirical fake script for HBO's Silicon Valley as a critique of the show's portrayal of programmers. He exaggerates the show's recurring tropes — Richard's stuttering, physical incompetence, and submissiveness to business people — to absurd extremes to highlight what he sees as the show's reductive and demeaning depiction of technical people. The parody escalates from stuttering to self-mutilation, making the subtext of the show's dynamics explicit and grotesque. quotes.quote: If Facebook is claiming to require real names and identities on its platform, and requiring people to signup for Facebook to eliminate impostors, then they are extorting signups out of people.
quotes.summary: Zed Shaw describes his experience with someone impersonating him on Facebook and Facebook's refusal to take action. He was forced to create his own Facebook account just to report the impostor, which he argues constitutes extortion of signups. Facebook's response was bureaucratic deflection rather than resolution. He argues that Facebook's claim to enforce real-name identity policies while failing to remove impostors makes them liable for any resulting slander. He contends that reporting abuse on a platform should not require membership on that platform.
quotes.key_insight: Platforms that claim to enforce real-name identity policies but require membership to report impostors are effectively extorting signups while enabling the very abuse they claim to prevent.

quotes.quote: Some things that you need to do are a lot like arsenic. These are activities that you know you should do, but the act of doing them simply drives you mad and feels like they are killing you.
quotes.summary: Shaw introduces the concept of 'Educational Mithridatism' — deliberately building tolerance for boring but necessary practice, named after the king who immunized himself against poison. He argues that hating repetitive exercises like scales or flashcards is completely normal, not a character flaw, and rejects both the puritanical worship of mindless work and the bohemian rejection of rote learning. The ideal approach combines rote training for foundational skills with creative application. Shaw provides a concrete method: establish a baseline tolerance, incrementally increase exposure time, take breaks, and periodically test whether the practice is paying off. The core message is that the ability to endure productive boredom is a trainable skill, not an innate virtue.
quotes.key_insight: The ability to endure necessary but boring practice is not an innate character trait but a trainable skill, and framing it as poison you can build immunity to removes the shame and provides a concrete path forward.

quotes.quote: You can either write software or you can't. I'm not saying that people who can are special. Hell, total absolute complete fucking idiots with dried husks for skulls write code.
quotes.summary: Shaw argues that meritocracy, while appealing in theory as a way to judge people solely on skill, fails catastrophically in practice because it creates 'Mr. Teflon' — toxic employees who leverage one past achievement to become untouchable despite terrible behavior. He contrasts programming and painting as genuinely skill-based activities where competence is easy to verify, then shows how the meritocracy ideal gets weaponized to protect incompetent assholes. Shaw acknowledges the alternative — bureaucratic HR-driven organizations — has its own problems of credentialism, but at least provides mechanisms to remove toxic people. He concludes that self-employment is the only true meritocracy.
quotes.key_insight: Meritocracy in tech has been corrupted from a system that protects talented outsiders into one that shields toxic employees from consequences, making it just another form of workplace abuse.

quotes.quote: By attaching a personal emotional connection to what he's created he has shielded himself from criticism.
quotes.summary: Shaw argues that creators across poetry, art, and open source software use personal tragedy as a shield against legitimate criticism of their work. He observes this pattern in cafe poetry readings, abstract art installations, and open source projects, where attaching emotional meaning to mediocre work makes it immune to honest feedback. Shaw contends this is unfair to audiences and users, who deserve the right to genuine reactions. He advocates for accepting criticism when you publish work publicly, handling abusive people separately, and taking responsibility when your work has real problems.
quotes.key_insight: Using personal tragedy to shield your public work from criticism is a manipulative tactic that robs audiences and users of their right to honest response, whether the work is poetry, art, or open source software.

quotes.quote: They don't have enough experience to close the gap, but since they can describe what they want the function to do then that's their start.
quotes.summary: Shaw describes his teaching method of having beginners write comments first, then fill in code underneath—a technique he calls converting complex problems into 'paint-by-numbers' problems. He applies this same principle across disciplines: coding, writing, and painting all benefit from outlining before executing. He then proposes a code editor that would automate this process, using machine learning to suggest code beneath comments while continuously running tests. The editor would essentially formalize what programmers already do—searching Stack Overflow and documentation—into an integrated, intelligent workflow. Written in 2015, the idea presciently describes something close to modern AI coding assistants.
quotes.key_insight: Complex creative problems in any medium—code, writing, painting—are best solved by decomposing them into small, describable pieces and filling in the blanks, a process that machines could eventually automate.

quotes.summary: Shaw describes his teaching method of having beginners write comments first, then fill in code underneath—a technique he calls converting complex problems into 'paint-by-numbers' problems. He applies this same principle across disciplines: coding, writing, and painting all benefit from outlining before executing. He then proposes a code editor that would automate this process, using machine learning to suggest code beneath comments while continuously running tests. The editor would essentially formalize what programmers already do—searching Stack Overflow and documentation—into an integrated, intelligent workflow. Written in 2015, the idea presciently describes something close to modern AI coding assistants.

quotes.quote: You put free cookies out with turds in them because a sentient fly demands turds, and then you charge people for cookies without turds? That's fucking insane.
quotes.summary: Shaw uses an extended satirical dialogue to criticize open source software projects that cater to difficult community members at the expense of regular users. The allegory portrays FLOSS maintainers as bakers who put 'turds' (bad design decisions) in free cookies to appease a loud, obnoxious 'sentient fly' (toxic power users), while charging others for the clean version. The piece skewers the defensive posture maintainers take when receiving criticism, hiding behind the 'it's free' defense and bureaucratic contribution processes. Shaw argues that offering something for free doesn't immunize it from criticism, and that prioritizing toxic community members over ordinary users is self-destructive. The story ends with the café empty except for the fly—illustrating how this dynamic drives away the broader user base.
quotes.key_insight: Open source projects that prioritize appeasing their loudest toxic users over serving the broader community end up with an empty room and a product nobody else wants to use.

quotes.quote: You want to find out if your software works well? Have me try to use it.
quotes.summary: Shaw describes his seemingly supernatural ability to trigger software bugs in every system he touches, from Pearson's royalty payment system to Google Kubernetes to his bank's internal databases. He recounts a pattern spanning decades where routine actions like changing an address or adding bold text cause mysterious failures that support teams can't explain. He argues this curse actually made him a better programmer, since he learned to write defensively by assuming everything would break. He also criticizes companies for expecting users to file detailed bug reports and for getting hostile when bugs are pointed out.
quotes.key_insight: Constantly encountering software bugs as a user breeds defensive programming habits, but the tech industry's hostility toward bug reporters discourages the very feedback it needs most.

quotes.quote: After 6 months you can't really say someone is a beginner since, well, 6 months later is not the beginning.
quotes.summary: Shaw argues that the tech education world fails to distinguish between people who have never coded at all and those with a few months of experience, lumping both into 'beginner.' He proposes adopting terminology from art education: 'beginning coders' for people with zero experience, and 'early coders' for those who grasp syntax but struggle with problem-solving. Beginning coders need mechanical repetition to internalize concrete skills like syntax and typing, while early coders need guided projects to develop abstract problem-solving. Shaw attributes this blind spot to programmers' inability to visualize someone with zero knowledge, which he sees as a root cause of poor programming education.
quotes.key_insight: The tech world's failure to distinguish between someone with zero coding experience and someone with a few months of practice leads to educational materials that serve neither group well.

quotes.quote: What Americans seem to like is a form of impressionism that is 'correctly drawn, sloppily painted'.
quotes.summary: Shaw proposes a framework for categorizing paintings along two axes: liberal vs. conservative drawing, and liberal vs. conservative painting (color/application). He observes that American Impressionism typically features conservative (accurate) drawing with liberal (sloppy) painting, which he sees as a safe cliché. He maps out four quadrants — from hyperrealism (conservative/conservative) to abstract expressionism (liberal/liberal) — and plans a systematic painting project exploring all four dimensions using the same subject across four canvases.
quotes.key_insight: The tension between technical accuracy and expressive freedom in painting mirrors a broader cultural dynamic, and systematically exploring both axes can reveal which combinations produce genuinely interesting art versus comfortable clichés.

quotes.quote: Most of the painting programs are in that weird uncanny valley where they're close, but so irritatingly far that they feel awkward and weird.
quotes.summary: Shaw recounts his experience learning digital painting during a six-hour flight, comparing it to traditional painting. He argues that digital painting tools fundamentally misunderstand color mixing by presenting infinite color pickers instead of simulating how painters actually combine pigments from limited palettes. Despite this major flaw, he finds digital painting useful for portability and cost, and describes his process of following along with Richard Robinson's painting videos using ArtRage on a cheap graphics tablet. He draws a parallel between his painting learning method and his coding education philosophy: forcing yourself to work from memory rather than side-by-side copying.
quotes.key_insight: Digital painting tools fundamentally misunderstand painters by offering infinite color selection instead of simulating the constrained, subtractive process of mixing from a limited palette of primaries.

quotes.quote: They seem to think 'mixing' means choosing a color from all the possible colors. That's not how a painter works. I make new colors by combining colors from two piles of paint.

He argues that digital painting tools fundamentally misunderstand color mixing by presenting infinite color pickers instead of simulating how painters actually combine pigments from limited palettes. Despite this major flaw, he finds digital painting useful for portability and cost, and describes his process of following along with Richard Robinson's painting videos using ArtRage on a cheap graphics tablet. He draws a parallel between his painting learning method and his coding education philosophy: forcing yourself to work from memory rather than side-by-side copying.
quotes.key_insight: Digital painting tools fundamentally misunderstand painters by offering infinite color selection instead of simulating the constrained, subtractive process of mixing from a limited palette of primaries.

quotes.quote: I also end up copying Richard's mistakes and then learning how to fix them, which is really helpful.

quotes.quote: You saved the business of technology from the useless MBAs who did nothing but drive businesses into the ground on the backs of programmers like me.
quotes.summary: Zed Shaw writes an open letter to Paul Graham criticizing Hacker News as a platform that enables sustained harassment and abuse while YCombinator profits from it. He recounts seven years of repeated slander through anonymous accounts and recycled attack posts, arguing that YC selectively moderates only criticism of its own startups while allowing personal attacks on others. Shaw positions himself as someone with nothing to lose who could retaliate by publicly reviewing and criticizing YC startups, leveraging his large audience of coding students. He ultimately appeals to Graham to either shut down HN comments or heavily moderate them, arguing the experiment has run its course.
quotes.key_insight: Online platforms run by corporations with financial interests will always selectively moderate to protect their own investments while allowing unchecked abuse of outsiders who lack institutional power.

quotes.quote: Why the fuck am I just watching this?! I have paint and supplies right here. I could follow along!
quotes.summary: Shaw describes his evolution in how he uses painting instructional videos, moving from passive note-taking to actively painting along. After experiences with Bob Ross, Johannes Vloothius, and Richard Robinson — including an in-person workshop — he had the obvious realization that following along with paint in hand is far more effective than watching and taking notes. He argues this approach lets him always have a subject to paint and combines practice sessions with learning. The post concludes with ten practical tips for anyone wanting to paint along with instructional videos.
quotes.key_insight: Active practice alongside instruction — not passive consumption and note-taking — is the most effective way to learn a craft skill.

quotes.quote: I can't let feeling like a dork get in the way of progress in the things I love.
quotes.summary: Shaw introduces the concept of 'dork distance' — the unavoidable period of public incompetence that beginners must endure to develop any skill. He argues that mastery in painting, music, programming, or any practiced skill requires accumulating 'mileage' (miles of canvas, hours of practice, pages of code), and that this distance must be traveled while looking foolish. He uses his own unabashed dorkiness as proof that social embarrassment is survivable and even liberating. His practical advice: post your beginner attempts online under your real name or a pseudonym, endure the initial panic, and discover that the fear was imagined. The post is both a personal manifesto on embracing awkwardness and an educational philosophy about how fear of embarrassment is the primary barrier to learning.
quotes.key_insight: The unavoidable awkwardness of being a beginner — the 'dork distance' — is the real barrier to mastery, and the fastest way past it is to post your failures publicly until the fear loses its power.

quotes.quote: Negativity and criticism are an important part of creating anything of quality. You have to be critical of what you make or else you end up making total crap and never improve.
quotes.summary: Shaw argues against Pixar's 'plussing' culture — the idea that you should never criticize but only build on others' ideas. He contends that criticism is essential to producing quality work in any creative field, whether art, code, or writing. He points to Pixar's wage theft scandal as evidence that 'and then' cultures are really passive-aggressive power structures that suppress dissent. Shaw advocates a balanced approach: create freely in isolation first, then apply rigorous self-criticism before presenting work to teams. He rejects both toxic positivity and abusive criticism, arguing that honest feedback is what separates professionals from delusional amateurs.
quotes.key_insight: Cultures that ban criticism don't eliminate negativity — they just drive it underground where it becomes manipulation, power abuse, and exploitation by those at the top.

quotes.quote: All I want is a checkbox when I create my 'team' that reads: 'Members pay $_____ to join.'
quotes.summary: Shaw writes an open letter to Slack proposing a simple feature: a paid membership checkbox for Slack teams. He wants to use Slack as a platform to teach coding, where students would pay to join his Slack team, with Slack handling all payment processing and taking a cut. He argues Slack already has every feature needed except the ability to invert who pays, and frames it as a mutual business opportunity that would save him from dealing with payment and membership management overhead.
quotes.key_insight: Platforms like Slack sit on untapped creator-economy potential that could be unlocked by simply inverting their payment model to let members pay creators through the platform.

quotes.quote: Voyeur Marketing is an attempt to subvert the market by being the first one to offer a consumer some products so they don't see your competitors'.
quotes.summary: Shaw argues that 'Voyeur Marketing' — the practice of using personal data from social networks to target ads — is fundamentally flawed because knowing someone's private life doesn't predict purchases as well as knowing their past purchases. He contends that the distance between a life event and an actual purchase is mediated by research and brand loyalty, making personal data statistically invalid as a purchase predictor. He contrasts Amazon's effective recommendation engine (based on purchase history) with Twitter and Facebook's ineffective ad targeting (based on social behavior). Shaw predicts social networks will eventually pivot to building direct marketplaces to compete with Amazon, closing the gap between social activity and actual transactions.
quotes.key_insight: Surveillance-based advertising is fundamentally outcompeted by purchase-history targeting because private life events are poor predictors of buying behavior compared to what someone actually bought before.

quotes.quote: Entrepreneurs and artists now share that incredible sweet spot in our society of being given positions of great status for doing so little.
quotes.summary: Shaw draws a satirical parallel between artists and entrepreneurs, arguing both groups occupy inflated positions of status in society while primarily serving the ultra-wealthy. He mocks the idea from The Atlantic that 'entrepreneurs are the new artists' by showing how both groups share traits of self-importance, entitlement, and a belief they deserve free money simply for existing. Through sections on sexiness, world-changing ambitions, entitlement, and importance, he argues both groups produce work that ultimately benefits wealthy patrons while claiming to serve society. He criticizes Molly Crabapple's call for universal basic income as self-serving artist entitlement rather than genuine concern for the poor. The piece concludes that both artists and entrepreneurs have been granted undeserved social status for 'doing so little.'
quotes.key_insight: Artists and entrepreneurs are mirror images of the same entitlement — both claim world-changing importance while functioning as status-seeking servants of the ultra-wealthy.

quotes.quote: I cannot fathom how a group of people who are supposedly so intelligent and geared toward rational thought can hold in their head the idea that I can be wrong, and also right at the same time.
quotes.summary: Zed Shaw announces he's removing his 'Deconstructing K&R C' section from Learn C the Hard Way after years of backlash from the C programming community. Despite his goal of teaching people to critically analyze code—even code written by famous programmers—he was met with hostility from pedants who admitted he was technically correct but refused to accept his critique of their heroes. He reframes the book as a course in writing secure C code and rigorous programming analysis, while subtly encouraging readers to move on to newer languages like Go, Rust, and Swift. Shaw views the episode as emblematic of a toxic programming culture that prioritizes hero worship and trivia memorization over genuine education and improvement.
quotes.key_insight: Programming culture's hero worship and gatekeeping through trivia memorization actively prevents honest critique of established code and harms efforts to teach newcomers how to write safer software.

quotes.quote: Yes, all you people living in colder places are so much tougher than anyone living in San Francisco. Sure. Totally.
quotes.summary: Shaw recounts a plein air painting session at Baker Beach in San Francisco, where he painted a small oil study before the rising tide forced him to leave. He describes his compact 'cutie kit' setup that allows him to hold a palette and paint while standing. He critiques his own work, noting the water came out too green and lacked reflected sky light. The post focuses on practical adjustments he's making to his portable painting kit, including reducing brushes and limiting his palette to four colors for faster setup in challenging outdoor conditions.
quotes.key_insight: Constraints like weather, tides, and portability drive practical innovations in a plein air painting practice, pushing toward simpler setups and more efficient workflows.

quotes.quote: I remember one company I worked at had an employee who routinely scanned people's files looking for juicy bits of information he could view.
quotes.summary: Shaw proposes a detailed consumer privacy law that would require companies to disclose which employees can access user accounts, maintain visible access logs, and let users block specific employees from viewing their data. The proposal includes mandatory credential-access disclosures at login screens, monthly access activity notifications, and requirements that company agents identify themselves when interacting with users. Shaw acknowledges the proposal is technically feasible but politically impossible, as companies have no incentive to reveal their internal access practices. He concludes with cynical realism that even if consumers had this information, most wouldn't act on it.
quotes.key_insight: The basic security practice of logging and disclosing who accesses user accounts is technically trivial but will never be implemented because companies profit from the opacity.

quotes.quote: Sorry nerd, you don't have neck tattoos and heroin track marks so you can't possibly be doing art.
quotes.summary: Shaw argues that the traditional art world's gatekeeping over what counts as 'art' is hypocritical, since conceptual art already stretches the definition to absurdity. He proposes an alternative definition: art is any activity that induces a state of flow requiring altered brain function. Drawing on neuroscience research about how music engages the whole brain differently, he extends this to programming, writing, painting, and dancing. He suggests flow may explain why people enjoy creative activities as a form of altered consciousness similar to meditation or religious experience. He concludes that art education should focus on teaching students to achieve flow states rather than just producing external results.
quotes.key_insight: Art should be defined not by what it produces but by the flow state it induces in the creator—a neurological altered consciousness that programming, painting, music, and writing all share.

He concludes that art education should focus on teaching students to achieve flow states rather than just producing external results.
quotes.key_insight: Art should be defined not by what it produces but by the flow state it induces in the creator—a neurological altered consciousness that programming, painting, music, and writing all share.

quotes.quote: Most just take my books, teach my material, then rip off students for $15k programs that don't actually get anyone deployed.
quotes.summary: Zed Shaw announces an upcoming series of daily posts exposing problems with coding bootcamps. While acknowledging bootcamps' goal of making coding education accessible, he argues most are exploitative, charging $15k while using his free materials and employing unqualified instructors. He describes receiving reports from students about abuse, sexual harassment, and false effectiveness claims, noting students are afraid to speak out for fear of damaging their careers. He pledges to separate criticism of schools from their students and offers to help bootcamp graduates find work.
quotes.key_insight: Coding bootcamps exploit students' fear of unemployment to charge premium prices for substandard education while silencing criticism through students' dependence on the school's reputation.

quotes.quote: The insanity of the internet is not that there are trolls, it's that trolls are given full rights to abuse others and people defend the troll's right to be abusive fucking assholes.
quotes.summary: Shaw examines how internet social norms protect trolls while punishing their victims, using a Guardian article about an author who tracked down her Goodreads troll as a launching point. He argues that the 'Don't Feed The Trolls' ethos mirrors classic abuser dynamics, giving trolls full license to harass while penalizing any response from victims. He then expands into a broader hierarchy of creative social status, ranking trolls at the top and nerds at the bottom based on what society lets each group get away with. Drawing from his own experience as someone who occupies multiple roles—troll of trolls, artist, author, musician, coder, and nerd—he illustrates how each identity carries different social privileges and penalties. The overall argument is that internet culture has created perverse incentive structures where the most destructive actors enjoy the most protection.
quotes.key_insight: Internet culture has constructed social norms that grant trolls more protection and social power than any creative class, mirroring the dynamics of real-world abusive relationships where victims are punished for fighting back.

quotes.quote: The internet abuser will send out a small pin prick, hoping for a response so they can justify their abuse because the social norms of the internet gives them license to then attack if you respond.

quotes.quote: It sounds like total pseudo-science bullshit, but my right hand had learned the difference between 'writing' and 'drawing', and it wanted to be in charge again.
quotes.summary: Zed Shaw recounts the exact day he learned to draw — March 10, 2013 — during an intensive drawing course based on Betty Edwards' 'Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain.' He describes bizarre euphoric giggling fits while doing blind contour drawings with his left hand, a sensation no one else he knew had experienced. During a five-day intensive class taught by Edwards' son Brian, Shaw felt a breakthrough on the third day while drawing piers in perspective at Fort Mason. The experience launched an obsession with visual art that led him to teach himself painting and drawing across multiple mediums over the following 19 months, defying the belief instilled in him as a child that nerds aren't creative.
quotes.key_insight: Learning to draw is a perceptual breakthrough that can happen at a specific, identifiable moment — and the cultural belief that some people simply aren't artistic is a damaging lie.
